{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>

'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>

Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>

'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>

== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."

Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. {{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref>
