'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref>

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref>

== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.

'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社}}

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|12=draft}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏|12=draft}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏|12=draft}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直|12=draft}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|12=draft}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|12=draft}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=draft}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|12=draft}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連|12=draft}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記|12=draft}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|12=draft}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|12=draft}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|12=draft}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主|12=draft}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇|12=draft}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|12=draft}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬|12=draft}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|12=draft}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|12=draft}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|12=draft}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|12=draft}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|12=draft}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|12=draft}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|12=draft}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|12=draft}} <!--Nakatomi no Ikatsu-->

Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|12=draft}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|12=draft}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|12=draft}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主|12=draft}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇|12=draft}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|12=draft}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬|12=draft}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|12=draft}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|12=draft}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|12=draft}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|12=draft}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|12=draft}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|12=draft}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|12=draft}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|12=draft}} <!--Nakatomi no Ikatsu-->

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|12=draft}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏|12=draft}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏|12=draft}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直|12=draft}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|12=draft}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|12=draft}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|12=draft}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命|12=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|12=draft}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連|12=draft}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記|12=draft}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|12=draft}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|12=draft}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|12=draft}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主|12=draft}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇|12=draft}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|12=draft}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬|12=draft}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|12=draft}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|12=draft}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|12=draft}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|12=draft}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|12=draft}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|12=draft}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|12=draft}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|12=draft}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|12=draft}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 雷大臣命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[仲哀天皇]]、[[神功皇后]]、[[允恭天皇]]？ | 氏族 = [[河内国]][[中臣氏|中臣連]]、[[壱岐氏|壱伎直]]、[[山城国]]呉公、[[摂津国]]神奴連、[[摂津国]][[生田氏|生田首]]、[[中臣栗原氏|中臣栗原連]]、[[摂津国]][[対馬国|津島直]]、[[河内国]]三間名公祖<ref name="#1">『[[新撰姓氏録]]』</ref> | 父母 = 父：[[臣狭山命]] <ref name="#2">『[[尊卑分脈]]』</ref><ref name="#3">『松尾社家系図』（『続群書類従』巻第181所収）</ref> | 兄弟 = | 妻 = 百済国女（あるいは新羅国女） | 子 = [[大小橋命]]、[[真根子命]]、弟子命<ref name="#1"/>、日本大臣命<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> | 特記事項 = }} '''中臣 烏賊津 '''（なかとみ の いかつ）は『[[日本書紀]]』等に伝わる[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]・'''[[中臣氏|中臣連]]'''の祖。『[[古事記]]』に記載はない。別名に'''中臣烏賊津使主'''（なかとみのいかつおみ）、'''雷大臣命'''（いかつおみのみ）。『[[日本書紀]]』ではその系譜は明らかではないが、『[[続群書類従]]』所収の『[[松尾大社|松尾社家系図]]』によれば、[[臣狭山命]]の子で[[大小橋命]]・[[真根子命]]の父であるとするため、『[[尊卑分脈]]』に見える「'''跨耳命'''」と同一人物であると考えられる<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/>。『日本書紀』では「中臣」を冠するが、烏賊津使主の時代には中臣と名乗っていなかったとされる<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/>。
== 記録 == 『[[日本書紀]]』巻第八によると、[[仲哀天皇]]の急逝に際し、 {{quotation|皇后<small>（きさき）</small>及び[[大臣 (古代日本)|大臣]]（おほおみ）[[武内宿禰]]（たけしうち の すくね）、天皇<small>（すめらみこと）</small>の喪<small>（みものおもひ）</small>を匿<small>（しな）</small>めて、天下<small>（あめのした）</small>に知<small>（し）</small>らしめず}}
そのようにした上で、皇后の気長足姫（のちの[[神功皇后]]）は・中臣烏賊津[[連]]・[[大三輪大友主]][[キミ (カバネ)|君]]（おおみわ の おおともぬし の きみ）・[[物部胆咋]]連（もののべ の いくい の むらじ）、[[大伴武以]]連（おおとも の たけもつ の むらじ）に、「天皇がなくなったことを[[百姓]]（＝人民）に知らせてはならない」と断った上で、百寮を率いさせ、宮中を守らせた、という<ref>『日本書紀』仲哀天皇9年2月5日条</ref>。
『書紀』巻第九によると、皇后は、それから罪を払い、過ちを改めて、斎宮（いわいのみや）を定めた後で、一ヶ月後、吉日を選んで、斎宮に入って、自分から神主となり、武内宿禰に命じて琴をひかせ、「中臣烏賊津使主」（なかとみ の いかつ の おみ）を呼んで、[[審神者]]（さにわ＝神慮を審察する人）とした。そして、
{{quotation|「先<small>（さき）</small>の日<small>（ひ）</small>に天皇<small>（すめらみこと）</small>」に教<small>（をし）</small>へたまひしは誰<small>（いづれ）</small>の神<small>（かみ）</small>ぞ。願<small>（ねが）</small>はくは其<small>（そ）</small>の名<small>（みな）</small>をば知<small>（し）</small>らむ」}}
7日7夜かけて、ついにその答えがあった、という<ref>『日本書紀』神功皇后摂政前紀（仲哀天皇9年）3月1日条</ref>。「中臣」とは「中つ臣」の略で、神と人との仲介をするという意味なのだが、その名にふさわしい功績をあげている。
なお、『書紀』十三によると、その後、允恭天皇の朝廷にも[[舎人]]の「中臣烏賊津使主」という人物が登場するのであるが、同名異人であるとも、伝承の混乱であるとも言われている。『[[新撰姓氏録]]』の中臣志斐連、神奴連の系譜では、[[天児屋命]]からの世代数にはそれぞれ若干の異同があり、烏賊津に該当する人物は、「伊賀津」と「雷大臣命」の2名になるが、壱伎直、生田首の雷大臣は世代数や系図からも[[崇神天皇]]の二世代前の人物である[[伊香津臣命]]（伊香迹臣命）であり、類似した名称を持つ人物である。[[飯田武郷]]の「日本書紀通釈」は時代と身分が異なるとしており、[[河村秀根]]の「書紀集解」は祖父の名をもって、舎人として仕えた、としている。
その内容は、皇后[[忍坂大中姫]]の妹、[[衣通郎姫]]を呼ぶ使いの役割を果たしたいきさつである<ref>『日本書紀』允恭天皇7年12月1日条</ref>。
さらに、『[[続日本紀]]』巻第三十六にでは、[[781年]]、[[栗原子公|栗原勝子（くりはらのすぐりこ）]]という人が、自分たちの先祖の「伊賀津臣」が神功皇后の時に百済人の女性と子をなした、という話を朝廷に言上している。この書においては[[天御中主命]]の二十世孫とされ、[[臣狭山命|意美佐夜麻]]の子と記される<ref>『続日本紀』桓武天皇 天応元年7月16日条</ref>。
『[[尊卑分脈]]』では「初めて卜部姓を賜う」、「雷大臣命は、足中彦天皇の朝廷のとき、大兆の道を習い、亀卜の術に達し、卜部の姓を賜りその事で供奉せしむ」とある。
== 対馬での伝承 == [[対馬]]には雷大臣命に関する伝承がいくつか存在する<ref>鈴木棠三『対馬の神道』（三一書房、1972年）</ref>。 *[[厳原町]]豆酘の[[雷神社]]は[[新羅]]征討からの帰還後に雷大臣命が邸宅を構えた場所であり、雷大臣命はそこで朝鮮からの入貢を掌り、祝官として祭祀の礼や亀卜の術を伝えたという。 *厳原町阿連の[[雷命神社]]の社家である橘家は雷大臣命の末裔である。 *[[美津島町]]加志の[[太祝詞神社]]には雷大臣命の墓がある。 *[[上対馬町]]大増の[[霹靂神社]]は雷大臣命が新羅から帰還したときに上陸した地（浜久須）である。 *上対馬町芦見の[[能理刀神社]]は雷大臣命が亀卜を行った場所である。
== 系譜 == 父は[[臣狭山命]]で、子に[[大小橋命]]、[[壱岐氏|伊岐宿禰]]の祖・[[真根子命]]がいる。また[[百済]]（『[[神社明細帳]]』では新羅）の女を娶って栗原連の祖・[[日本大臣命]]を生んでいる<ref name="#1"/>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * [[宇治谷孟]]訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、1988年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、1992年 * 『続日本紀』5 新日本古典文学大系16 岩波書店、1998年 * [[佐伯有清]]編『日本古代氏族事典【新装版】』[[雄山閣]]、2015年 * [[岸俊男]]編『日本の古代7 まつりごとの展開』[[中公文庫]]、1996年
== 関連項目 == * [[大神神社]]

{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 雷大臣命 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 別名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = | 主君 = [[仲哀天皇]]、[[神功皇后]]、[[允恭天皇]]？ | 氏族 = [[河内国]][[中臣氏|中臣連]]、[[壱岐氏|壱伎直]]、[[山城国]]呉公、[[摂津国]]神奴連、[[摂津国]][[生田氏|生田首]]、[[中臣栗原氏|中臣栗原連]]、[[摂津国]][[対馬国|津島直]]、[[河内国]]三間名公祖<ref name="#1">『[[新撰姓氏録]]』</ref> | 父母 = 父：[[臣狭山命]] <ref name="#2">『[[尊卑分脈]]』</ref><ref name="#3">『松尾社家系図』（『続群書類従』巻第181所収）</ref> | 兄弟 = | 妻 = 百済国女（あるいは新羅国女） | 子 = [[大小橋命]]、[[真根子命]]、弟子命<ref name="#1"/>、日本大臣命<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> | 特記事項 = }} '''中臣 烏賊津 '''（なかとみ の いかつ）は『[[日本書紀]]』等に伝わる[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]・'''[[中臣氏|中臣連]]'''の祖。『[[古事記]]』に記載はない。別名に'''中臣烏賊津使主'''（なかとみのいかつおみ）、'''雷大臣命'''（いかつおみのみ）。『[[日本書紀]]』ではその系譜は明らかではないが、『[[続群書類従]]』所収の『[[松尾大社|松尾社家系図]]』によれば、[[臣狭山命]]の子で[[大小橋命]]・[[真根子命]]の父であるとするため、『[[尊卑分脈]]』に見える「'''跨耳命'''」と同一人物であると考えられる<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/>。『日本書紀』では「中臣」を冠するが、烏賊津使主の時代には中臣と名乗っていなかったとされる<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/>。
== 記録 == 『[[日本書紀]]』巻第八によると、[[仲哀天皇]]の急逝に際し、 {{quotation|皇后<small>（きさき）</small>及び[[大臣 (古代日本)|大臣]]（おほおみ）[[武内宿禰]]（たけしうち の すくね）、天皇<small>（すめらみこと）</small>の喪<small>（みものおもひ）</small>を匿<small>（しな）</small>めて、天下<small>（あめのした）</small>に知<small>（し）</small>らしめず}}
そのようにした上で、皇后の気長足姫（のちの[[神功皇后]]）は・中臣烏賊津[[連]]・[[大三輪大友主]][[キミ (カバネ)|君]]（おおみわ の おおともぬし の きみ）・[[物部胆咋]]連（もののべ の いくい の むらじ）、[[大伴武以]]連（おおとも の たけもつ の むらじ）に、「天皇がなくなったことを[[百姓]]（＝人民）に知らせてはならない」と断った上で、百寮を率いさせ、宮中を守らせた、という<ref>『日本書紀』仲哀天皇9年2月5日条</ref>。
『書紀』巻第九によると、皇后は、それから罪を払い、過ちを改めて、斎宮（いわいのみや）を定めた後で、一ヶ月後、吉日を選んで、斎宮に入って、自分から神主となり、武内宿禰に命じて琴をひかせ、「中臣烏賊津使主」（なかとみ の いかつ の おみ）を呼んで、[[審神者]]（さにわ＝神慮を審察する人）とした。そして、
{{quotation|「先<small>（さき）</small>の日<small>（ひ）</small>に天皇<small>（すめらみこと）</small>」に教<small>（をし）</small>へたまひしは誰<small>（いづれ）</small>の神<small>（かみ）</small>ぞ。願<small>（ねが）</small>はくは其<small>（そ）</small>の名<small>（みな）</small>をば知<small>（し）</small>らむ」}}
7日7夜かけて、ついにその答えがあった、という<ref>『日本書紀』神功皇后摂政前紀（仲哀天皇9年）3月1日条</ref>。「中臣」とは「中つ臣」の略で、神と人との仲介をするという意味なのだが、その名にふさわしい功績をあげている。
なお、『書紀』十三によると、その後、允恭天皇の朝廷にも[[舎人]]の「中臣烏賊津使主」という人物が登場するのであるが、同名異人であるとも、伝承の混乱であるとも言われている。『[[新撰姓氏録]]』の中臣志斐連、神奴連の系譜では、[[天児屋命]]からの世代数にはそれぞれ若干の異同があり、烏賊津に該当する人物は、「伊賀津」と「雷大臣命」の2名になるが、壱伎直、生田首の雷大臣は世代数や系図からも[[崇神天皇]]の二世代前の人物である[[伊香津臣命]]（伊香迹臣命）であり、類似した名称を持つ人物である。[[飯田武郷]]の「日本書紀通釈」は時代と身分が異なるとしており、[[河村秀根]]の「書紀集解」は祖父の名をもって、舎人として仕えた、としている。
その内容は、皇后[[忍坂大中姫]]の妹、[[衣通郎姫]]を呼ぶ使いの役割を果たしたいきさつである<ref>『日本書紀』允恭天皇7年12月1日条</ref>。
さらに、『[[続日本紀]]』巻第三十六にでは、[[781年]]、[[栗原子公|栗原勝子（くりはらのすぐりこ）]]という人が、自分たちの先祖の「伊賀津臣」が神功皇后の時に百済人の女性と子をなした、という話を朝廷に言上している。この書においては[[天御中主命]]の二十世孫とされ、[[臣狭山命|意美佐夜麻]]の子と記される<ref>『続日本紀』桓武天皇 天応元年7月16日条</ref>。
『[[尊卑分脈]]』では「初めて卜部姓を賜う」、「雷大臣命は、足中彦天皇の朝廷のとき、大兆の道を習い、亀卜の術に達し、卜部の姓を賜りその事で供奉せしむ」とある。
== 対馬での伝承 == [[対馬]]には雷大臣命に関する伝承がいくつか存在する<ref>鈴木棠三『対馬の神道』（三一書房、1972年）</ref>。 *[[厳原町]]豆酘の[[雷神社]]は[[新羅]]征討からの帰還後に雷大臣命が邸宅を構えた場所であり、雷大臣命はそこで朝鮮からの入貢を掌り、祝官として祭祀の礼や亀卜の術を伝えたという。 *厳原町阿連の[[雷命神社]]の社家である橘家は雷大臣命の末裔である。 *[[美津島町]]加志の[[太祝詞神社]]には雷大臣命の墓がある。 *[[上対馬町]]大増の[[霹靂神社]]は雷大臣命が新羅から帰還したときに上陸した地（浜久須）である。 *上対馬町芦見の[[能理刀神社]]は雷大臣命が亀卜を行った場所である。
== 系譜 == 父は[[臣狭山命]]で、子に[[大小橋命]]、[[壱岐氏|伊岐宿禰]]の祖・[[真根子命]]がいる。また[[百済]]（『[[神社明細帳]]』では新羅）の女を娶って栗原連の祖・[[日本大臣命]]を生んでいる<ref name="#1"/>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』(一)、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * [[宇治谷孟]]訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、1988年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀』全現代語訳（上）、講談社学術文庫、1992年 * 『続日本紀』5 新日本古典文学大系16 岩波書店、1998年 * [[佐伯有清]]編『日本古代氏族事典【新装版】』[[雄山閣]]、2015年 * [[岸俊男]]編『日本の古代7 まつりごとの展開』[[中公文庫]]、1996年
== 関連項目 == * [[大神神社]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なかとみ の いかつ}} [[Category:中臣氏|いかつ]] [[Category:日本神話の人物]] [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:日本古代の武人・武官]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|qq=draft}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏|qq=draft}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏|qq=draft}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直|qq=draft}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命|qq=draft}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連|qq=draft}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記|qq=draft}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|qq=draft}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft}}.
== Records ==
According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主|qq=draft}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}, Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}, Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ingyō|ja|允恭天皇|qq=draft}} mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|qq=draft}}</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬|qq=draft}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|qq=draft}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|qq=draft}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|qq=draft}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|qq=draft}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|qq=draft}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|qq=draft}}

| clan = {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi clan|qq=}} (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki clan|qq=}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta clan|qq=}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi Kurihara clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima clan|qq=}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=}}</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#2">*{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonpi Bunmyaku|qq=}}</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainishi no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi clan|qq=}} from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}. Although not mentioned in the *{{ill|Records of Ancient Matters|ja|古事記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Records of Ancient Matters|qq=}}*, he is referred to in the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chronicles of Japan|qq=}}* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *{{ill|Sonpi Bunmyaku|ja|尊卑分脈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonpi Bunmyaku|qq=}}* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}.

According to the *{{ill|Chronicles of Japan|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chronicles of Japan|qq=}}* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Chūai|qq=}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jingū|qq=}} kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Ikatsu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|qq=}}, and others. == Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on {{ill|Tsushima Island|ja|対馬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima Island|qq=}}:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raimyo Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tai Norito Shrine|qq=}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hekireki Shrine|qq=}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norito Shrine|qq=}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|ja|大神神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|qq=}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]] (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki clan|qq=}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta clan|qq=}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi Kurihara clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima clan|qq=}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#2">*[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainishi no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi clan|qq=}} from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. Although not mentioned in the *[[:en:Kojiki|Records of Ancient Matters]]*, he is referred to in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]]* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}.
== Records ==
According to the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]]* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Ikatsu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|qq=}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō]] mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on [[:en:Tsushima Island|Tsushima Island]]:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raimyo Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tai Norito Shrine|qq=}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hekireki Shrine|qq=}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norito Shrine|qq=}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]

Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on [[:en:Tsushima Island|Tsushima Island]]:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raimyo Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tai Norito Shrine|qq=}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hekireki Shrine|qq=}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norito Shrine|qq=}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]</ref>

== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.

{{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]] (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki clan|qq=}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta clan|qq=}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi Kurihara clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima clan|qq=}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#2">*[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainishi no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi clan|qq=}} from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. Although not mentioned in the *[[:en:Kojiki|Records of Ancient Matters]]*, he is referred to in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]]* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}.
== Records ==
According to the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]]* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Ikatsu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|qq=}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō]] mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on [[:en:Tsushima Island|Tsushima Island]]:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raimyo Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tai Norito Shrine|qq=}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hekireki Shrine|qq=}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norito Shrine|qq=}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]</ref>

== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.

Genealogical texts such as *[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}.
== Records ==
According to the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]]* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Ikatsu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|qq=}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō]] mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on [[:en:Tsushima Island|Tsushima Island]]:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raimyo Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tai Norito Shrine|qq=}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hekireki Shrine|qq=}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norito Shrine|qq=}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]

'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi clan|qq=}} from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. {{Infobox noble | name = Ikatsu no Mikoto | image = | image_size = | caption = | era = [[Kofun period]] | birth_date = Unknown | death_date = Unknown | other_names = Nakatomi no Ikatsu, Ikatsu Omimi, Rai Daijin no Mikoto | honorary_title = | deity_name = | posthumous_name = | burial_place = | Rank = | lord = [[Emperor Chūai]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Emperor Ingyō]]? | clan = [[:en:Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi clan]] (Kawachi Province), {{ill|Iki clan|ja|壱岐氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iki clan|qq=}}, Wu nobility in Yamashiro Province, Shinetsu clan in Settsu Province, {{ill|Ikuta clan|ja|生田氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikuta clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Settsu Province), {{ill|Nakatomi Kurihara clan|ja|中臣栗原氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi Kurihara clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tsushima clan|ja|津島直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Settsu Province), Mimami clan (Kawachi Province)<ref name="#1">*[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]</ref> | parents = Father: {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}<ref name="#2">*[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]</ref><ref name="#3">Matsuo Shrine Genealogy (in *{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 群書類従}}*, vol. 181)</ref> | siblings = | spouse = Woman of Baekje (or Silla) | children = {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Dainishi no Mikoto|ja|弟子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainishi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#1"/>, {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="#2"/><ref name="#3"/> }}
'''Ikatsu no Mikoto''' (中臣 烏賊津) is a legendary noble and regarded as the founder of the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|ja|中臣連|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中臣氏}} from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]]. Although not mentioned in the *[[:en:Kojiki|Records of Ancient Matters]]*, he is referred to in the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]]* under various names such as '''Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi''' and '''Rai Daijin no Mikoto'''. Genealogical texts such as *[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]* identify him as the son of Omi Sayama no Mikoto and father of {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}} and {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}.
== Records ==
According to the *[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]]* (vol. 8), upon the sudden death of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] kept his death secret with the help of senior officials, including {{ill|Nakatomi no Ikatsu|ja|中臣烏賊津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Ikatsu|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|ja|大三輪大友主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa no Ōtomonushi|qq=}}, and others. Ikatsu no Mikoto was tasked with protecting the court during this period.<ref>*[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], Emperor Chūai, year 9, February 5 entry.</ref>
Later, in vol. 9 of the *Chronicles*, Empress Jingū chose Ikatsu no Mikoto to perform sacred divinations (審神者) during her preparation for an important ritual.<ref>*[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Chronicles of Japan]], Empress Jingū, pre-regency record (Emperor Chūai, year 9, March 1 entry).</ref> His role as a mediator between gods and humans is emphasized, reflecting the meaning of the term "Nakatomi."
Subsequent records in the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ingyō|Emperor Ingyō]] mention a courtier named Nakatomi no Ikatsu Omimi, who is believed by some scholars to either be a different person with the same name or the same individual as Ikatsu no Mikoto.<ref>*[[:en:Sonpi Bunmyaku|Sonpi Bunmyaku]]</ref>
== Tsushima Legends ==
Several legends about Ikatsu no Mikoto are preserved on [[:en:Tsushima Island|Tsushima Island]]:<ref>Tsuzuki Tōzō, *Shinto in Tsushima* (San-Ichi Shobō, 1972).</ref> * {{ill|Raijin Shrine|ja|雷神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raijin Shrine|qq=}} in Izuhara Town commemorates Ikatsu no Mikoto as the figure who managed foreign tribute after returning from Silla. * {{ill|Raimyo Shrine|ja|雷命神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raimyo Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Izuhara is linked to his descendants. * {{ill|Tai Norito Shrine|ja|太祝詞神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tai Norito Shrine|qq=}} in Mitsushima Town is believed to house his grave. * {{ill|Hekireki Shrine|ja|霹靂神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hekireki Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} marks the place of his arrival from Silla. * {{ill|Norito Shrine|ja|能理刀神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Norito Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is where he is said to have performed divinations.
== Genealogy ==
Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
== See also == * [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]

Ikatsu no Mikoto is identified as the son of {{ill|Omi Sayama no Mikoto|ja|臣狭山命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi Sayama no Mikoto|qq=}}. His children include {{ill|Ōkogashi no Mikoto|ja|大小橋命|1=Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkogashi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbashi no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Maneko no Mikoto|ja|真根子命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maneko no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|ja|日本大臣命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Daijin no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Some accounts mention his marriage to a woman from Baekje or Silla, with their descendants linked to several prominent clans.<ref>*[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * *Chronicles of Japan*, Vol. 1, Iwanami Bunko, 1994. * Ujitani Takeshi (trans.), *Chronicles of Japan*, Kodansha Academic, 1988. * Saeki Arikiyo, *Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans*, Yuzankaku, 2015. * Kishi Toshio (ed.), *Ancient Japan: Government and Ritual*, Chūō Kōron Bunko, 1996.
